questions.question: What triggers the fight-or-flight response in your body?

options.text: The prefrontal cortex sends signals to relax
The amygdala signals the hypothalamus, activating the sympathetic nervous system
The hippocampus releases calming hormones
The cerebellum controls involuntary muscle tension

questions.explanation: The amygdala (your brain's alarm center) sends signals to the hypothalamus, which activates your sympathetic nervous system, triggering the cascade of physical changes we experience as anxiety.

questions.question: Why does your heart race when you feel anxious?

options.text: Your body is malfunctioning due to stress
Your body is pumping blood to muscles to prepare for fight or flight action
Your heart is weakened by anxiety
It's a random symptom with no biological purpose

questions.explanation: Your racing heart is your body doing exactly what it's designed to do—pumping blood to your muscles in preparation for physical action (fighting or fleeing). This is a survival mechanism, not a malfunction.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the 'Four F's' of stress responses?

options.text: Fight
Freeze
Focus
Fawn

questions.explanation: The four F's are Fight (aggression), Flight (escape), Freeze (immobilization), and Fawn (people-pleasing). 'Focus' is not one of the recognized stress responses.

questions.question: According to the lesson, when does anxiety become 'problematic'?

options.text: Whenever you feel any anxiety at all
When the alarm fires too often, too intensely, at inappropriate times, or persists too long
Only when you have panic attacks
When other people notice your symptoms

questions.explanation: Anxiety becomes problematic not because the alarm system exists, but when it fires too often, too intensely, at inappropriate times, or persists too long after the threat has passed.

questions.question: What is the helpful metaphor used in the lesson to describe anxiety?

options.text: A broken alarm that needs to be fixed
An oversensitive smoke alarm that goes off for burnt toast
A computer virus that needs to be deleted
A character flaw that needs to be eliminated

questions.explanation: The lesson compares anxiety to an oversensitive smoke alarm. The alarm isn't broken—it's just calibrated too sensitively. The goal isn't to disable it (you need it for real fires) but to recalibrate it to respond appropriately.

title: What Anxiety Is: Fight-or-Flight and Normalizing Symptoms - Quiz
